sentences.text: We were talking about Wally Cohn.
Right, so--
Yeah, my special assistant.
He was worth his weight in gold, I'm telling you.
On the first day he reported in, I said, Wally, I said-- we had these huge, old rooms in that Justizpalast that were about 30 feet by 30 feet.
This is in the courthouse.
In the courthouse.
And I had to put in 25 translators to work on tables in there.
And the light was terrible.
Now Germany in the wintertime is dark anyway.
And from the center of the ceiling would hang one 20-watt bulb.
Now you just cannot work like that, you know?
And so I said, Wally, I said, go out and get me reading lamps.
He said, how many?
I said, I don't know.
I said, you just start them coming in, and I'll tell you when to stop.
It was a terrible assignment, because this was a destroyed Germany.
You couldn't buy anything.
Money was useless.
I said, reading lamps-- that's your first assignment.
So [LAUGHS] this was about 9:00 in the morning.

So, about noon, two doors down the hall from me was this group of British-- Wrens, I think they called them-- they were female army types, you see-- under the command of a very willowy, aristocratic British young lady called-- one of these hyphenated names-- Smyth-Jones or something like that.
And I was-- at lunchtime, Lieutenant Smyth-Jones came into my office.
She said, oh, Commander, I have something very strange has happened.
And I said, what?
And she says, well, you know you've told us we're never to leave the room completely empty, so there'd be somebody to answer the phone.
I said, that's right.
She said, I told all the other girls to go to lunch.
And I was-- walked over to the window, and I was looking out there, and I was smoking a cigarette.
And, she said, after I put my cigarette out, I turned around, and  you know what?
She said, all my reading lamps were gone.
[LAUGHS]
I bet you I know what happened.
I said, you go back to your room, and let me handle it from here.
I called Wally in.
I said, Wally, look, boy.
I said, you've got to take those lamps back to those people.
He says, but they're limeys, Commander, they're limeys!
I said, yep, but they're limeys on our side, boy.
[LAUGHS] Then he goes-- but that-- now, he-- as I say, he-- and one of the other things that he could-- that we couldn't get was typewriters.
So that scoundrel, he stomped around, and somewhere he found out that there was an entire warehouse in Munich full of typewriters-- German typewriters.
And the owner was holding them out, waiting for better market conditions before letting them go.
Well, all we needed to know was that they were in that warehouse.
Then we could get military government, you see, to move in and grab them.
I mean, Wally was simply invaluable.
I used to give-- the only thing we could do there was give parties.
There was no amusement.
And I was-- it would be my turn to give a party, and so I would call Wally and say, Wally, I'm going to have 35 people out at the house for a Friday-night party.
And I said, take care of it and get things organized for me.
So I get home from the Justizpalast about 5:30, and there'd be a three-piece German band tuning up.
The chef would have got trays of hors d'oeuvres laid out.
The lawn had been mowed.
There were Japanese lanterns hanging from the trees.
I mean, you know, it was a beautiful orient-- there were people were dancing on the patio, and everyone was having a grand time.
And so, [INAUDIBLE] and Monday morning, I'd call Wally in.
I'd say, well, that was a terrific party you arranged.
Now, what do I owe you?
He says, Sir, he says, you owe me $200, 600 marks, and four cartons of cigarettes.
[LAUGHS] Did everyone come to these parties?
I mean, all nationalities, or who came?
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Well, we were-- you know, if they invited us, we invited them.
That was the deal.
Mostly it was British, American, and a few French.
We-- very rarely would the-- we would invite the Russians, but they would never accept.
Once in a while.
The-- [LAUGHS] the Soviet-- head of the Soviet KGB took a shine to a girl whom I knew quite well-- as a matter of fact was my-- sort of my-- was a sister of my brother-in-law.
That's what she was.
And he was trying desperately to seduce her.
And she had told me that she found him repulsive, and she was keeping him at arm's length and so forth.
And we were invited to-- we would be invited to parties.
And I would go and-- and squire her.
And-- so we would start to dance, and then he would have his minions cut in, and then he would cut in, and so forth and so on.
And then she would give me this leer.
And I-- anyway.
Occasionally, but only because this one greasy Russian type was chasing this female.
And--
I mentioned Sprecher, the-- the guy who had this [GERMAN] in New York, from Brooklyn.
Then did I mention Frank-- Hans Frank?
He was British national, but he--
Hans.
I think.
No, Wolf.
Wolf Frank.
Wolf Frank.
And--
Born in Germany.
And Jewish-- Jewish background.
He got out in time.
But his German was perfect.
His English was perfect.
And he was an extremely valuable and extremely able young man.
I don't know.
He was English-- British.
He went back to Britain.
And I knew this female-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hillary Gascon-- that did And we were good friends.
Matter of fact, I had expected to see her here, but at the last minute she couldn't come.
Anyway.
And she told me that he had had some difficulties after returning to Britain after the end of the trial, and eventually committed suicide.
I don't know why.
Prisoners?
We had-- we had translators who had been prisoners.
I don't recall any interpreters.
Now, don't forget, every German youth at one point had to become a member of the Hitlerjugend, regardless.
And some of the German people may have been members of the Hitlerjugend, but there were no Nazi party members as such.
No.
No.
Yes.
That was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the end of October, 1946.
Well, there was this one with this terrible-- terrible concentration camp speaker that Virginia [?
Grey ?] got fouled up with.
Then there was a--
And then there was another one.
We had a very smart-- Gilbert-- Major Gilbert, a psychologist.
Yes.
Who-- when we showed movies-- you've seen movies of the concentration camps.
We had them all.
Oh, yes.
And the-- the Signal Corps, Army Signal Corps, had taken all these pictures, you see.
And then they were shown.

And the Major-- Major Gilbert fixed this up-- I'll give you the-- the defendant was sitting here, and he put small lights in front of each of them, something like footlights, and would turn them on fairly low.
And they shone up onto the faces of the defendants.
Now, when he darkened the room to show these movies, he turned on those lights.

And at the same time, he trained movie cameras from across the room onto their faces, to get their reactions of these terrible, horrible, unbelievable scenes as they were played out on the-- on the screen.
These are the things that haunted my nightmares for months-- oh!
You've probably seen some of them, haven't you?
I'm sure over here at this Holocaust museum they have them or have many of them.
And I remember that-- that psychiatrist was a very smart character.
Very smart.
Now what about the decision?
How was it read?
How did the interpreters handle that?
The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the decision was drawn up by the judges, individually.
And the press-- particularly the French press-- was very anxious to get advance copies of this.
And we-- I was given the material and told to translate it in advance into French, Russian, German, and so on.
But under no circumstances let the press get advance copy of it.
Now they--
Yes.
Yes.
The judges.
Lord Justice Lawrence, himself.
Well, now, that's another point.
So what am I going to do?
I mean, we had heard that the French Agence Presse had offered $10,000 for an advance copy.
Yes.
And now this is a real bad-- what do I say-- temptation for an underpaid translator, friend.
So what I did-- I had everybody-- I found a-- a small German barracks over in Furth.
And--
Next town to-- to Nuremberg.
And we-- I-- I told-- I got the translators together-- interpreters together-- translators together.
And I said, I want a dozen of you to volunteer to be sequestered for two weeks.
I said, we're going to put you in prison.
We're going to let nobody out.
I said, we're going to have guards around.
I said, we'll feed you, we'll show you movies, and so forth, but that's it.
I said, we've got to keep this private while it's being worked on.
And they agreed.
And they signed little chits, you know, agreeing to do it.
So they went over to this thing, and-- for two weeks.
And I got a picture of that.
At the end of that, they're handing me this completed translation.
Then that was read in court.
And then, that was without the sentence-- [INAUDIBLE] guilty or not guilty, but no sentence.
Then, I think it was two or three days later, the court came in and gave each man his sentence.
No.
No.
No.
Oh yes.
We got the same court guards, in the white caps and everything, that guarded the courtroom, guarded that building, 24 hours a day.
Not personally.
But I was told by several interpreters that the word was, out at press camp, that 10-- they could-- 10 grand-- if they'd give an entire copy they'd get 10 grand
Yes.
Yes.
Most of the day.
Most of the day.
No.
Yes.
Well, of course, they all expected the sentences.
But the sentences didn't come until a day or two later.
But they could all see, well, this is guilty, this is not guilty, and so forth.
But let me tell you one more story.
The-- when these sentences were read, it became clear that three people were going to be acquitted-- Schacht, von Papen, and-- and Fritzsche, I think, was the third one.
Anyway.
But von Papen-- no, no-- Schacht is the one I want to talk about.
Now, von Schacht had been Hitler's finance minister.
Now, I don't know whether you realize it, but there is quite a group of US Schachts.
And, as matter of fact, Schacht himself was born in Brooklyn-- a member of a group-- of a family that had emigrated from Germany to the United States and then back-- emigrated back.
All right.
He, Schacht, was brought up in Brooklyn and spent, I think, the first 11 years of his life on the streets of Brooklyn, New York.
And he spoke, of course, [LAUGHS] perfect American English, you see?
But, of course, he wouldn't admit to anything but-- but German in the court.
And he was a smart apple, and he figured out in advance-- well before the end of the war-- what was going to happen.
And so he went around among the Nazis, and he found-- took all the documents that would incriminate him and destroyed them.
Well, [SIGH] that's what everybody guessed, because nobody-- he wouldn't admit it, of course.
And so-- and he was free.
Now, [LAUGHS] then he went downstairs to the-- they have a little press room-- and they said that he would be available with the other guys to-- to have a press conference, you see, after--
After the decision.
Before the sentencing.
And so I went down.
I took in the press conference.
And the-- the-- they had them, the three of them, lined up there on a long table, and microphones in front of them.
And they got down to Schacht, and Schacht said, now, gentlemen-- he said, I am a grandfather.
And when I leave here, I have no place to go except to my daughter's home, and she'll take me in, I'm sure.
But she has small children, and I am their beloved Grandpa-- Opa, in German.
And I have never gone to visit them without taking them something in the way of a present.
He says, [INAUDIBLE] I have nothing except the clothes I stand in.
Now, he said, if you gentlemen would be willing to cooperate with me and contribute a few packs of cigarettes or some candy bars or something, I'd be glad to-- to cooperate with you.
Well the next thing-- [LAUGHS] there was this pile of PX loot, you see, in front of him, on-- on the table next to his microphone.
I said to myself, that old son of a bitch.
[LAUGHS] Here he is-- he's been five minutes released from the court, and he's in business already.
You know.
[SIGHS] No, they all made a-- made a fetish of acting stony-faced.
I don't recall.
Nobody broke down or wept or anything that I can recall.
I-- I was done.
I was finished.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] No, no, I'm sorry.
I was still involved in this getting the record set up for print.
Yeah.
The court had appointed me as chairman of a committee to see if the court record couldn't be printed permanently.
In all languages.
And so I went around, and I found-- there was a-- we had a-- there was a press in Nuremberg that could do this.
And I found some people, and I put them to work under Mrs.
Wolf, my beloved Marguerite Wolf.
And--
Say it again?
Yes.
And, see, things like this would happen.
A guy would stand up in court, and he would say, 'In the year nineteen hundred and forty-six'-- or let's say, '30-- anyway, he'd get his numbers backwards and wouldn't realize that he had misspoken.
Any-- any speaker.
Every speaker could do this.
And so, preparing the text for print, these people would go through and carefully check everything.
Did you mean to say that?
And then go back and check it with the speaker.
Yeah, yeah-- to the speaker.
Did you mean--?
Oh, gosh, no, I didn't realize I'd made that mistake, you see.
Or they would find an obvious grammatical [? failure-- ?] failure.
Or they would find a place where an interpreter had made an obvious goof.
And it had to be cleaned up for print.
It wasn't-- it wasn't fit for print.
Now, wait.
So I-- I supervised the group.
I-- I set up the group that did that.
And then, after it was done, then we had to print the damned thing.
And this is where Sieg Roth came in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sieg says, well, let me handle it.
I've done things like that in New York.
I said, well, all right.
And so we-- we had to get-- we had paper-- excuse me.
We had a printing press in Erlangen, but we didn't have paper or binding materials.
We had to go to the Russian zone-- which was Eastern Germany, in those days-- and get that to be sent down.
And we had to get that through the Berlin control council.
And Sieg and I went to Berlin to-- to get this material, you see.
And on the way there, I stopped-- there was a little navy PX in Frankfurt.
We had a flyby at Frankfurt.
And they said, well, now, we're getting rid of all our cigars.
Would you like a couple of boxes?
Well, I said, I don't smoke cigars, but I go hunting in the woods, and the German woodsmen would love-- I said, yeah, give me about four boxes of cigars.
So I took these cigars and went to Berlin.
And I went down, and we had to see a whole bunch of people, and Sieg had to see some people.
And I came back at the end of the day.
And I got to my room, and Sieg [LAUGHS] was sitting there in front of these packages of $100 bills, like this.
And he pushed a whole bunch-- and he says, these are yours.
I said, mine?
He says, yeah.
He says, I sold your cigars.
I said, I didn't tell you to sell any goddamn cigars!
On the black market, you see?
[LAUGHS] He sold my cigars.
Anyway.
So we got-- we got the material.
And then we got down, and we got the-- we got set up.
And they set up the press and everything.
And Sieg, at one point, was trying to-- we were trying to see-- it was the best deal in Nuremberg-- would it be better if we went to Munich to have it printed, you see?
And I said, well, Sieg, go down to Munich and take a look.
And so he came back, and he says-- he says there is a German newspaper, in German, called I think, or something-- something-- anyway.
I read it.
It's superb.
Absolutely superb.
And, he says, I went to see them, and there was a Major Rosenthal-- army-- a US military government major in charge.
And he said, I asked him if he could take it up, and he said, yeah, he could do it for us.
And I asked him what he would charge.
He says, I wouldn't charge anything.
I said, charge nothing?
What do you mean?
Well, he says, this paper I'm running, he says, is on Hitler's press.
Now, Hitler owned this Nazi thing.
And he says, we have his 10-million-mark bank account.
And I said, what the hell better use could we put this to than to print the Nuremberg record?
[LAUGHS] So we went back to Nuremberg.
But the judges-- Lord Justice Lawrence was quite definite.
No, he says, we're not going to take tainted money.
He said, I don't want anybody to be able to say, at any time in the future, that this is a put-up job.
This has got to be absolutely above approach-- reproach [INAUDIBLE].
So we got-- sorry.
And then Sieg-- all of a sudden Sieg comes to me, and he says, everything's working except the bindery.
I said, what's the matter with the bindery?
Well, he says, the Germans have laws about how much you can pay people for various different jobs.
This is a hangover from the Nazi days.
And the bindery needs so-and-so-- I think 35 girls-- to work for 30 marks a week to do this simple-- these simple jobs in the bindery.
And I said, well, what's the matter with that?
Well, he says, every one of those girls can find a GI boyfriend who will give them a pack of cigarettes that's worth 100 marks in a week, so why the hell should they work?
So I says-- he says, we've got to get some PX goods to give them, in addition to these 30 marks.
So we went down to see the PX officer.
And I explained my point of view, and Sieg explained his point of view.
And the PX officer-- I have complete orders not to give any foreign nationals any PX materials.
And we walked out of there, and I said, Sieg, we didn't get anywhere on that deal.
I said, what are you going to do?
Well, I got to thinking about something else.
About two days later, I said, Sieg, whatever happened about those girls in the printing press?
He says, sir, we're printing books.
I said, huh?
[LAUGHS] I didn't ask him how he arranged it.
I didn't ask him what he had done.
But I'm sure there was some PX material.
How much time did it take to assemble the record?
Quite a while.
By the time I left-- there were-- there were 24 volumes of proceedings-- verbatim proceedings, from the trial-- the [INAUDIBLE] trial.
Then there were 20-- 20 volumes or 21 volumes--  I'm not sure-- of backup documents, supporting documents, which makes a total of 40-odd-- what was it? 42 or 43-- volumes altogether.
Now, we had to do 2,500 copies of this into German, 2,500 copies into French, 2,500 copies into Russian, 5,000 into English-- 2,500 for the British, 2,500 for the US.
You multiply that all out, friend, and you are talking about half a million volumes.
It was a huge job.
And it wasn't completed until about six months after the end of the trial.
Sieg sent me copies.
I would say May of '47.
In Philadelphia.
In Lansdowne.
In the fall of-- of a-- of a, '45. '46.
Well, go back to my academic career.
I-- they had asked me, didn't I want to stay in the navy?
Well, the answer was no, for two reasons.
First of all, everybody I ever knew in the military-- in them you know, foreign military-- was an ultra right-winger.
And anything I could not stand was having to work with some of these right-wingers.
And I didn't want that.
And then, in addition, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very much aware of the fact that my background was not-- well, let me put it this way.
The navy belonged to the Annapolis men, and I was not an Annapolis man.
And there was no question of me ever becoming anything like an [?
Annapolis. ?] So I wanted to go back to the academic world.
In September-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, it must have been December of '46.
Shortly thereafter.
About two weeks thereafter.
Then I went back to-- to the University of Pennsylvania, and I tried to pick up my PhD studies.
I had interrupted-- been interrupted seven years prior to that, remember?
And I thought I could just pick up where I left-- I had to start all over again.
That seven-year hiatus--
In '52, I think it was.
In German.
